walkthrough.title: Choose a Lost Species
walkthrough.text: Select one of the 6 Lost Species cards. Each represents a real animal that hasn't been seen in decades — from the Wondiwoi Tree Kangaroo to Wallace's Giant Bee. The chosen card determines which island map and clue configuration you'll use. Return the other Lost Species cards to the box.

walkthrough.title: Build the Island Map
walkthrough.text: Take the corresponding island board tiles and assemble them as shown on the Lost Species card's setup diagram. Each island is divided into habitat regions with different terrain types — jungle, mountain, coast, and wetland. The modular board means each game has a different geography.

walkthrough.title: Prepare Clue Cards and Tokens
walkthrough.text: Shuffle the clue deck for the chosen island. Place animal tokens on the board as indicated by the setup guide. These tokens represent known animal populations that help narrow down where the Lost Species might be hiding. Set the Search tokens nearby.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Expedition Board
walkthrough.text: Place the expedition board where all players can see it. This tracks the overall expedition progress, available actions, and the round counter. Place the round marker on space 1.

walkthrough.title: Distribute Player Components
walkthrough.text: Each player takes a scientist card, a set of sighting report tokens in their color, a player screen to hide their deduction notes, and a deduction sheet. Players also receive starting clue cards as indicated on their scientist card.

walkthrough.title: Deal Starting Clues
walkthrough.text: Deal private clue cards to each player as indicated by the player count rules. These clues give each scientist unique information about where the Lost Species is NOT located. Keep these hidden behind your player screen — sharing information strategically is key to the game.

walkthrough.title: Determine First Player
walkthrough.text: The player who most recently visited a national park goes first. Give them the first player marker. You're ready to begin the expedition!

walkthrough.title: Game Overview
walkthrough.text: The Search for Lost Species is a deduction game where players are scientists trying to locate a real endangered species on an island habitat. You gather clues, eliminate impossible locations, and report sightings to earn contribution points. The scientist who contributes most to finding the Lost Species wins.

walkthrough.title: Turn Structure
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you perform exactly one action from the available options: Search, Investigate, Report a Sighting, or use your Scientist's special ability. After your action, play passes clockwise. The game continues for a set number of rounds determined by the player count.

walkthrough.title: Search Action
walkthrough.text: Place a Search token on any region of the island. Then draw a clue card from the deck. This clue gives you information about the Lost Species' habitat — it might eliminate certain terrain types, regions, or proximity to other animals. Record this on your deduction sheet.

walkthrough.title: Investigate Action
walkthrough.text: Choose another player and ask them a yes-or-no question about one of their clue cards. For example: 'Does your clue eliminate the coastal regions?' The asked player must answer truthfully. This lets you gather information without spending a Search action, but it also reveals your line of thinking to opponents.

walkthrough.title: Report a Sighting
walkthrough.text: Place one of your sighting report tokens on a specific region where you believe an animal (or the Lost Species itself) is located. If you're reporting a regular animal sighting, you score points based on accuracy. If you're reporting the Lost Species location, this is your big guess for major points.

walkthrough.title: Scientist Special Abilities
walkthrough.text: Each scientist card has a unique ability that can be used once per round. These range from gaining extra clues, peeking at other players' information, placing additional search tokens, or getting bonus points for correct sightings in specific terrain types. Use your ability wisely.

walkthrough.title: Clue Types and Deduction
walkthrough.text: Clues come in several types: Terrain clues (the species is/isn't in jungle, mountain, etc.), Proximity clues (the species is/isn't adjacent to a specific animal), Region clues (the species is/isn't in a named region), and Habitat clues (combining multiple conditions). Cross-reference clues on your deduction sheet to narrow possibilities.

walkthrough.title: Scoring Sightings
walkthrough.text: When you report a sighting, it's verified at the end of the game. Correct animal sightings earn 1-2 contribution points depending on difficulty. A correct Lost Species sighting earns 5 points. Incorrect sightings earn nothing. You have a limited number of sighting tokens, so make them count.

walkthrough.title: End of Game Trigger
walkthrough.text: The game ends after the final round is complete, or immediately when all players have placed all their sighting tokens. Some scenarios have additional end conditions based on the Lost Species card.

walkthrough.title: Final Scoring
walkthrough.text: Reveal the Lost Species' true location. Score all sighting reports: correct animal sightings, correct Lost Species identification, and any bonus points from scientist abilities or expedition milestones. The player with the most contribution points wins. In case of a tie, the player who correctly identified the Lost Species wins.

walkthrough.title: Meet the Expedition Team
walkthrough.text: Let's play through the first two rounds with three scientists: Alex (the Botanist, green), Blake (the Tracker, blue), and Casey (the Cartographer, yellow). We're searching for Wallace's Giant Bee on the island of Bacan in Indonesia. The island has been set up with 12 regions across 4 terrain types.

walkthrough.title: Alex's First Turn — Search
walkthrough.text: Alex chooses the Search action, placing a Search token on the Central Jungle region. Alex draws a clue card: 'The Lost Species is NOT in any coastal region.' This eliminates 3 of the 12 regions! Alex marks the coastal regions with X's on the deduction sheet.

walkthrough.title: Blake's First Turn — Search
walkthrough.text: Blake also Searches, placing a token on the Northern Mountain. Blake's clue reads: 'The Lost Species is NOT adjacent to the River Monitor.' Blake checks the board — the River Monitor token is in the Eastern Wetland, adjacent to 3 regions. Blake crosses those off.

walkthrough.title: Casey's First Turn — Investigate
walkthrough.text: Casey decides to Investigate instead of Search. Casey asks Alex: 'Does your clue eliminate any mountain regions?' Alex answers truthfully: 'No.' Now Casey knows the Lost Species could be in the mountains, and everyone at the table heard this exchange.

walkthrough.title: Player Mistake — Casey Forgets Deduction Notes
walkthrough.text: Casey got excited about the investigation result but forgot to update the deduction sheet! Always record every piece of information immediately — both from your own clues AND from investigations and other players' actions. Information you forget is points you lose.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Alex Investigates
walkthrough.text: Alex uses the Investigate action on Blake: 'Does your clue eliminate the Western Jungle?' Blake says 'No.' Combined with Alex's own coastal elimination, Alex is narrowing the search to inland jungle and mountain regions.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Blake Reports a Sighting
walkthrough.text: Blake feels confident and reports a sighting of the Sulawesi Bear Cuscus in the Southern Jungle. Blake places a blue sighting token there. This animal token is on the board, and Blake's clues suggest it matches this location.

walkthrough.title: Player Mistake — Blake Reports Too Early
walkthrough.text: Blake used a sighting token in round 2 on a regular animal — not the Lost Species. While this might score 1-2 points if correct, Blake now has one fewer token for the Lost Species identification, which is worth 5 points. Early sighting reports are risky; you often want to save tokens for when you have stronger deductions.

walkthrough.title: Round 2 — Casey Uses Special Ability
walkthrough.text: Casey's Cartographer ability allows looking at 2 clue cards from the deck without taking them. Casey peeks and sees: 'Not in wetland' and 'Adjacent to the Seram Bandicoot.' Casey records both pieces of info — wetland is eliminated, and the species must be near the bandicoot in the Central Mountains.

walkthrough.title: The Deduction Narrows
walkthrough.text: After just 2 rounds, the team has collectively eliminated coastal (Alex), wetland (Casey), and areas adjacent to the River Monitor (Blake). The remaining possibilities are jungle and mountain regions not adjacent to the River Monitor. Each player has slightly different information — that's the competitive tension.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Continue Your Expedition
walkthrough.text: You've seen the core loop: Search to gain clues, Investigate to learn from others, Report when confident, and use your Scientist ability for an edge. As the rounds progress, your deduction sheet fills up and the possible locations shrink. Time your Lost Species report carefully — too early risks a wrong guess, too late and someone else might beat you to it!

walkthrough.title: Keep Meticulous Notes
walkthrough.text: Your deduction sheet is your most valuable tool. Update it after every action — yours AND other players'. Note not just what's eliminated, but what questions were asked and what terrain types are still possible. A small notation error can lead you to the wrong region.

walkthrough.title: Balance Searching and Investigating
walkthrough.text: Searching gives you private information but costs an action. Investigating gives you information based on others' knowledge and is free of tokens, but reveals your thinking. Early game, Search to build your private knowledge base. Mid-game, Investigate to fill gaps without spending tokens.

walkthrough.title: Watch Other Players' Actions
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to where others place Search tokens and what questions they ask during Investigations. If Blake keeps searching mountain regions, Blake probably has clues pointing there. If Casey asks about coastal proximity, Casey might be narrowing down a coastal-adjacent area.

walkthrough.title: Save Sighting Tokens for High-Value Reports
walkthrough.text: You have limited sighting tokens. The Lost Species is worth 5 points versus 1-2 for regular animals. Don't waste tokens on low-confidence guesses early. Wait until you've narrowed the field significantly before committing tokens.

walkthrough.title: Use Your Scientist Ability Strategically
walkthrough.text: Each scientist ability is powerful but limited. Don't use it just because you can — save it for moments when the extra information or action will make a real difference in your deduction. The best time is often mid-game when you're close to narrowing down the location.

walkthrough.title: Don't Reveal Too Much
walkthrough.text: When answering Investigation questions, you must be truthful, but you can be strategic about what you investigate. Asking about a region you've already eliminated can throw others off your trail. Be aware that every question telegraphs your reasoning.

Small errors lead to wrong conclusions

Asking about eliminated regions can mislead

walkthrough.title: Information Asymmetry Exploitation
walkthrough.text: Track exactly which clues each player has seen. If you know Alex has seen 3 clues and you've heard 2 of their Investigation answers, you can deduce what their third clue likely covers. This meta-deduction lets you gain information without spending actions.

walkthrough.title: Misdirection Through Investigations
walkthrough.text: Ask Investigation questions about regions you've already eliminated. Other players will assume you're still considering those areas, buying you time while they pursue false leads. This works best in the mid-game when everyone is racing to narrow down.

walkthrough.title: Timing the Lost Species Report
walkthrough.text: The optimal time to report the Lost Species is when you're 80-90% confident and other players seem close. Waiting for 100% confidence often means someone else beats you. If two regions remain and you have 50/50 odds, consider whether the 5-point gamble is worth it versus guaranteed smaller sightings.

walkthrough.title: Efficient Clue Cross-Referencing
walkthrough.text: Combine terrain, proximity, and region clues to create compound eliminations. If you know the species isn't coastal AND isn't adjacent to Animal X, check if any inland regions are also adjacent to Animal X — those get double-eliminated, speeding up your deduction.

walkthrough.title: Player Count Adaptation
walkthrough.text: In 2-player games, you have more rounds and actions per player, so thorough deduction wins. In 4-5 player games, information spreads faster through Investigations, so speed and early commitment matter more. Adjust your Search-to-Investigate ratio based on player count.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Board State
walkthrough.text: As Search tokens accumulate on the board, they reveal collective knowledge. Regions with many Search tokens have been well-explored — the Lost Species is more likely in under-searched areas. Use this spatial information alongside your deduction sheet.

summary.title: Information Asymmetry Exploitation
bullets: Track which clues each player has seen

entries.definition: The target animal the expedition is searching for. Each game uses one of 6 real endangered species that haven't been seen for decades.
Victory points earned through correct sightings, Lost Species identification, and scientist bonuses. Most points wins.
Player's private tracking sheet for recording clue information and eliminating possible locations for the Lost Species.
Barrier that hides each player's deduction sheet and private clue cards from other players.
Limited tokens in each player's color used to report animal sightings or the Lost Species location on the board.
Tokens placed on regions during the Search action, indicating explored areas.
Card providing information about where the Lost Species is or isn't located. Types include terrain, proximity, region, and habitat clues.
A clue that eliminates or confirms a terrain type (jungle, mountain, coast, wetland) as the Lost Species' habitat.
A clue about the Lost Species' spatial relationship to other animal tokens on the board (adjacent or not adjacent).
A clue that directly eliminates or confirms a specific named region on the island map.
A compound clue combining multiple conditions to describe the Lost Species' location requirements.
Place a Search token and draw a clue card. Provides private information about the Lost Species' location.
Ask another player a yes/no question about their clue cards. They must answer truthfully.
Place a sighting token on a region to claim an animal is there. Verified at game end for scoring.
Unique power on each scientist card, usable once per round. Effects vary by character.
Modular board tiles assembled into the island map. Configuration determined by the chosen Lost Species card.
Central board tracking round progress, available actions, and expedition milestones.
Tokens on the board representing known animal populations. Used as reference points for proximity clues.
One of four terrain types on the island. Dense tropical forest habitat.
One of four terrain types. Elevated terrain with distinct habitat conditions.
One of four terrain types. Shoreline regions adjacent to the ocean.
One of four terrain types. Marshy, waterlogged habitat areas.
Two regions are adjacent if they share a border on the island map. Important for proximity clues.
Real-world conservation organization partnered with the game. Their Lost Species list inspired the game's theme.
Given to the starting player. Passes clockwise at the start of each new round.
